?Listen carefully and let the client explain the problem.
?Offer a concrete solution: a refund or compensation.
?Reassure the client that it is being handled.
?Apologize sincerely, even if it was not your fault.
?Follow through and confirm everything in writing.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

I am so sorry for the inconvenience.
Sinto muito pelo transtorno.
The issue is being handled right now.
O problema está sendo resolvido agora.
Your refund has been processed.
Seu reembolso foi processado.
You will be fully compensated.
Você será totalmente compensado.
Everything will be followed through today.
Tudo terá seguimento hoje.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A guest complains that their room is not ready. You say:
A I am so sorry -- the room is being cleaned right now.
B The room is cleaning, wait please.
C Not my problem, ask reception.
A client was charged twice. You reassure them:
A The duplicate charge has been cancelled and refunded.
B You charged two times yourself.
C Maybe the money comes back sometime.
You want to sound calm and professional. You say:
A The issue is being handled by our team.
B Someone is doing the issue now.
C The issue handles itself soon.
An angry client threatens a bad review. You respond:
A I hear you, and this is what can be done for you today.
B Please calm down, it is not serious.
C Write the review if you want.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine a client calls you, upset: their group's rooms were overbooked and no one apologized. Record a short service-recovery response. Apologize for the inconvenience, use the passive voice to explain what is being done ('the rooms are being arranged', 'you will be refunded'), reassure them, and offer one goodwill gesture. Never argue. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1I am so sorry for the inconvenience.Sinto muito pelo transtorno.
2The problem is being handled right now.O problema está sendo resolvido agora.
3Your refund has already been processed.Seu reembolso já foi processado.
4As a goodwill gesture, this is on us.Como cortesia, isso é por nossa conta.
5You have my word -- I will follow through today.Tem a minha palavra -- vou dar seguimento hoje.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Negotiating Partnerships and Contracts

How to negotiate a partnership and report what was agreed. Key words: contract, terms, clause, to negotiate, partnership, to sign off on, deadline, draft, to renew, binding, to meet halfway, leverage. Structure: reported speech (backshift the tense, tell + person vs say, reported questions with if/whether).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Contract -- a formal written agreement between two sides (contrato)
"We signed the contract last week."
Terms -- the conditions of an agreement (termos, condicoes)
"The terms are good for both companies."
Clause -- one specific section of a contract (clausula)
"There is a clause about cancellations."
To negotiate -- to discuss in order to reach an agreement (negociar)
"We negotiated a better rate."
Partnership -- a business relationship between two companies (parceria)
"This partnership will grow every year."
To sign off on -- to give final approval to something (aprovar formalmente)
"My director signed off on the deal."
Deadline -- the latest time by which something must be done (prazo)
"The deadline for the draft is Friday."
Draft -- an early version of a document (rascunho, minuta)
"I will send you the first draft today."
To renew -- to extend an agreement for more time (renovar)
"We renewed the contract for two years."
Binding -- legally forcing you to do something (vinculante, obrigatorio)
"Once signed, the contract is binding."
To meet halfway -- to compromise so both sides win a little (chegar a um meio-termo)
"Let us meet halfway on the price."
Leverage -- an advantage that gives you power in a negotiation (poder de barganha)
"Volume gives us leverage in the deal."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Contract
Terms
Clause
To negotiate
Partnership
To sign off on
Deadline
Draft
To renew
Binding
To meet halfway
Leverage

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Andrea is negotiating a two-year partnership with Anders. After the meeting, she confirms every point using reported speech. 'You said the rate would be fixed for two years,' she reminds him, 'and you told me the deadline was the end of March.' Anders replies that his director asked if she could commit to more volume. Andrea offers to meet halfway. Every point they agree is written into a clause in the draft; once both sides sign off, the contract is binding and the partnership can renew for years.

1. 'He said the rate would be fixed' reports which sentence?
A 'The rate will be fixed.'
B 'The rate is fixed right now.'
C 'Fix the rate, please.'
2. To report a question, you:
A keep the inversion: 'asked could we meet'.
B use if/whether with no inversion: 'asked if we could meet'.
C just add a question mark.
3. Which sentence uses 'tell' correctly?
A He told me the deadline was Friday.
B He told the deadline was Friday.
C He said me the deadline was Friday.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

Como relatar o que alguém disse ao confirmar um acordo (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
Recuar 1 passo / Backshift one stepwill->would, is->was, has->had.He said it would start Monday.
say vs telltell + pessoa (told me); say sem pessoa. tell needs a person.She told me / She said that...
Perguntas reportadas / Reported questionssem inversão; use if/whether ou wh-.He asked if we could meet halfway.
Regra de ouro / Golden rulerelate o que foi dito, um tempo verbal atrás.You said the rate would be fixed.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"He said the rate be fixed."
"She me the deadline was Friday."
"They asked we could meet halfway."
"Let us meet on the volume."
"Once we both sign , the deal is binding."
"We renewed the for two years."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma boa negociação na ordem correta.

?Ask what the other side really needs.
?Write every agreed point into a clause.
?Confirm what was said, using reported speech.
?Offer to meet halfway on the key point.
?Both sides sign off to make it binding.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

You said the rate would be fixed for two years.
Você disse que a tarifa ficaria fixa por dois anos.
My director told me the deadline was Friday.
Meu diretor me disse que o prazo era sexta-feira.
Can we meet halfway on the volume?
Podemos chegar a um meio-termo no volume?
Once we both sign off, the deal is binding.
Assim que ambos aprovarmos, o acordo é vinculante.
I will confirm all the terms in writing.
Vou confirmar todos os termos por escrito.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A partner promised a fixed rate last week. You confirm it:
A You said the rate would be fixed for two years.
B You say the rate will be fixed for two years.
C You said the rate is fixed two years.
You report your director's condition to the partner. You say:
A My director told me we needed forty room nights.
B My director said me we need forty room nights.
C My director told we need forty room nights.
You report the partner's question. You say:
A They asked if we could lower the deposit.
B They asked could we lower the deposit.
C They asked if could we lower the deposit.
You want to compromise on the volume. You say:
A Let us meet halfway on the room nights.
B Meet halfway room nights you must.
C The room nights meet in the half.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you have just finished negotiating a partnership. Record a short summary for your director. Report what the partner said and asked, using reported speech ('He said the rate would be fixed', 'They asked if we could...'), explain where you agreed to meet halfway, and say what the next step is (draft, then sign off). Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1You said the rate would be fixed for two years.Você disse que a tarifa ficaria fixa por dois anos.
2My director told me the deadline was Friday.Meu diretor me disse que o prazo era sexta-feira.
3Can we meet halfway on the volume?Podemos chegar a um meio-termo no volume?
4Once we both sign off, the deal is binding.Assim que ambos aprovarmos, o acordo é vinculante.
5I will confirm all the terms in writing today.Vou confirmar todos os termos por escrito hoje.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Presenting Your Company at a Trade Fair

How to pitch your company and describe it with relative clauses. Key words: to showcase, elevator pitch, to stand out, flagship, track record, selling point, to specialize in, cutting-edge, established, brochure, prospect, turnover. Structure: relative clauses (who / which / that / where; defining vs non-defining).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

To showcase -- to present something proudly to an audience (mostrar, exibir com orgulho)
"At the fair, we showcase our best destinations."
Elevator pitch -- a short, persuasive summary of your business (discurso rapido de venda)
"Her elevator pitch lasts thirty seconds."
To stand out -- to be clearly better or different from others (destacar-se)
"Great service is what makes us stand out."
Flagship -- the most important, best product or brand (carro-chefe)
"Patagonia is our flagship destination."
Track record -- a history of past results and achievements (historico de resultados)
"We have a strong track record with groups."
Selling point -- a feature that makes people want to choose you (diferencial, ponto forte)
"Flexibility is our main selling point."
To specialize in -- to focus on one particular area (especializar-se em)
"We specialize in luxury group travel."
Cutting-edge -- very modern and advanced (de ponta, de ultima geracao)
"We use cutting-edge booking technology."
Established -- existing for a long time and well respected (consolidado, estabelecido)
"We are a well-established agency."
Brochure -- a printed booklet with information about a company (folheto, catalogo)
"Take a brochure from our stand."
Prospect -- a possible future client (cliente em potencial)
"Every prospect who visits gets a demo."
Turnover -- the total amount of business a company does in a period (faturamento)
"Our turnover grew last year."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

To showcase
Elevator pitch
To stand out
Flagship
Track record
Selling point
To specialize in
Cutting-edge
Established
Brochure
Prospect
Turnover

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

At the fair, a prospect stops at Andrea's stand. 'We are an agency that specializes in luxury group travel,' she says. 'Our flagship, which is Patagonia, stands out every season.' She points to the cutting-edge platform where clients compare packages in real time. 'We are a well-established company that has a strong track record,' she adds, handing him a brochure. Every prospect who visits gets a demo -- and that is exactly why Escape Turismo's turnover keeps growing.

1. 'An agency that specializes in travel' -- 'that' refers to:
A a thing (the agency).
B a place.
C a person.
2. Which sentence uses commas correctly (non-defining clause)?
A Patagonia which is our flagship sells out.
B Patagonia, which is our flagship, sells out.
C Patagonia, that is our flagship, sells out.
3. Which relative word describes a place?
A who
B which
C where

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Relative Clauses

GRAMMAR

Como descrever pessoas, coisas e lugares numa mesma frase (explicacao em ingles e portugues).

FormUse / UsoExample
who / that (pessoas)para pessoas; info essencial, sem virgulas. for people.The team that runs our groups.
which / that (coisas)para coisas; info essencial, sem virgulas. for things.A package that includes flights.
where (lugares)para lugares. for places.The region where we specialize.
Non-defining / info extracom virgulas; nunca use 'that'. commas, never 'that'.Patagonia, which is our flagship, sells out.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"We are an agency specializes in luxury travel."
"Every prospect visits our stand gets a brochure."
"Patagonia, is our flagship, sells out."
"This is the region we specialize."
"What makes us out is our service."
"Let me our portfolio for you."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de um bom pitch de empresa na ordem correta.

?Say what your company specializes in.
?Name the selling point that makes you stand out.
?Hand over a brochure and offer to follow up.
?Showcase your flagship and your track record.
?Greet the prospect and open with a one-sentence pitch.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

We are an agency that specializes in luxury travel.
Somos uma agencia que se especializa em viagens de luxo.
Our flagship, which is Patagonia, sells out every season.
Nosso carro-chefe, que e a Patagonia, esgota toda temporada.
What makes us stand out is our service.
O que nos faz se destacar e o nosso servico.
This is the platform where clients compare packages.
Esta e a plataforma onde os clientes comparam pacotes.
Let me showcase our portfolio for you.
Deixe-me mostrar o nosso portfolio para voce.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A prospect asks what your company does. You say:
A We are an agency that specializes in group travel.
B We are an agency who specializes in group travel.
C We are an agency where specializes in group travel.
You describe your flagship with extra information. You say:
A Patagonia, which is our flagship, sells out fast.
B Patagonia, that is our flagship, sells out fast.
C Patagonia which is our flagship sells out fast.
You describe the people who book with you. You say:
A Every prospect who books gets a manager.
B Every prospect which books gets a manager.
C Every prospect where books gets a manager.
You describe the place where clients compare packages. You say:
A This is the platform where clients compare packages.
B This is the platform who clients compare packages.
C This is the platform which clients compare there.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine a prospect has just stopped at your stand at a trade fair. Record your thirty-second elevator pitch for Escape Turismo. Describe your company and portfolio using relative clauses ('We are an agency that...', 'Our flagship, which is...', 'the platform where...'), name one clear selling point, and invite the prospect to follow up. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1We are an agency that specializes in luxury travel.Somos uma agencia que se especializa em viagens de luxo.
2Our flagship, which is Patagonia, sells out every season.Nosso carro-chefe, que e a Patagonia, esgota toda temporada.
3What makes us stand out is our service.O que nos faz se destacar e o nosso servico.
4Every prospect who visits our stand gets a brochure.Todo cliente em potencial que visita nosso stand recebe um folheto.
5Let me showcase our portfolio for you.Deixe-me mostrar o nosso portfolio para voce.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Talking About Trends and the Future of Tourism

How to discuss trends and forecast the future with the future continuous and future perfect. Key words: trend, forecast, emerging, sustainable, demand, to boom, shift, eco-friendly, to predict, mainstream, niche, wellness tourism. Structure: future continuous (will be + -ing) vs future perfect (will have + past participle).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Trend -- a general direction in which things are changing (tendencia)
"Wellness travel is a growing trend."
Forecast -- a statement about what will probably happen (previsao)
"The forecast shows strong demand next year."
Emerging -- starting to develop and become important (emergente)
"Vietnam is an emerging destination."
Sustainable -- able to continue without harming the environment (sustentavel)
"Travelers want more sustainable options."
Demand -- how much people want to buy something (demanda, procura)
"Demand for eco-tours is rising fast."
To boom -- to grow or succeed very quickly (crescer explosivamente)
"Adventure travel is booming."
Shift -- a change in position, direction, or focus (mudanca, virada)
"There is a shift toward local experiences."
Eco-friendly -- not harmful to the environment (ecologico)
"We now offer eco-friendly hotels."
To predict -- to say what you think will happen (prever)
"Experts predict a record season."
Mainstream -- accepted by or common among most people (predominante, comum)
"Remote work has gone mainstream."
Niche -- a small, specialized part of the market (nicho)
"Luxury safaris are a profitable niche."
Wellness tourism -- travel focused on health and well-being (turismo de bem-estar)
"Wellness tourism is our fastest-growing segment."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Trend
Forecast
Emerging
Sustainable
Demand
To boom
Shift
Eco-friendly
To predict
Mainstream
Niche
Wellness tourism

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

A journalist asks Andrea for her forecast. 'The biggest trend is sustainability,' she says. 'By 2030, most travelers will have chosen an eco-friendly trip, and demand will still be growing.' She explains that wellness tourism is booming: 'This time next year, we will be selling more retreats than beach packages.' What was a niche is going mainstream, and emerging destinations will have taken a big share. 'Companies that predict this shift early,' she adds, 'will be the ones leading the industry.'

1. 'By 2030, demand will have doubled' uses which form, and why?
A Future perfect -- the action is finished before 2030.
B Future continuous -- the action is in progress in 2030.
C Present simple -- it is a fact now.
2. Which signpost goes with the future continuous?
A by 2030
B this time next year
C last year
3. Which sentence is correct?
A This time next year, we will be running eco-tours.
B This time next year, we will run eco-tours.
C This time next year, we will have run eco-tours.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Future Continuous & Future Perfect

GRAMMAR

Como descrever o futuro com precisao (explicacao em ingles e portugues).

FormUse / UsoExample
Future continuouswill be + -ing: em andamento num ponto futuro. in progress at a future point.This time next year, we will be running tours.
Future perfectwill have + participio: concluido antes de um ponto futuro. finished before a future point.By 2030, demand will have doubled.
Marcadores / Signposts'by + data' = perfect; 'this time next year' = continuous.By then, it will have changed.
Regra de ouro / Golden ruleescolha a forma pelo marcador de tempo. let the time signpost decide.By 2030 (perfect) vs next year (continuous).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"By 2030, demand doubled."
"This time next year, we running eco-tours."
"The biggest right now is sustainability."
"What was a niche is going ."
"Experts a record season."
"Wellness tourism is ."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma boa previsao de mercado na ordem correta.

?Say what has already started to change.
?Forecast what will have happened by a future date.
?Look at the data and spot the main trend.
?Describe what you will be doing this time next year.
?Finish with one bold, confident prediction.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

By 2030, demand will have doubled.
Ate 2030, a demanda tera dobrado.
This time next year, we will be running eco-tours.
Nesta epoca no ano que vem, estaremos oferecendo eco-tours.
The biggest trend right now is sustainability.
A maior tendencia agora e a sustentabilidade.
What was a niche is quickly going mainstream.
O que era um nicho esta se tornando predominante.
Emerging markets will have taken a big share.
Os mercados emergentes terao conquistado uma grande fatia.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A journalist asks about demand by 2030. You forecast:
A By 2030, demand will have doubled.
B By 2030, demand will double.
C By 2030, demand doubles.
You describe your agency's activity next year. You say:
A This time next year, we will be offering retreats.
B This time next year, we will offer retreats.
C This time next year, we offer retreats.
You describe emerging markets by 2030. You say:
A By then, emerging markets will have taken a share.
B By then, emerging markets will taking a share.
C By then, emerging markets take a share.
You describe a trend that is becoming common. You say:
A What was a niche is going mainstream.
B What was a niche is going niche.
C What was a mainstream is going niche.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine a journalist has asked you to forecast the future of tourism. Record a one-minute forecast for Escape Turismo. Name the biggest trend, use the future perfect to say what will have changed by 2030 ('demand will have doubled'), use the future continuous to say what you will be doing next year ('we will be running...'), and finish with one bold prediction. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1By 2030, demand will have doubled.Ate 2030, a demanda tera dobrado.
2This time next year, we will be running eco-tours.Nesta epoca no ano que vem, estaremos oferecendo eco-tours.
3The biggest trend right now is sustainability.A maior tendencia agora e a sustentabilidade.
4What was a niche is quickly going mainstream.O que era um nicho esta rapidamente se tornando predominante.
5My forecast is that wellness tourism will keep booming.Minha previsao e que o turismo de bem-estar continuara crescendo.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Leading and Wrapping Up a Business Meeting

How to open, steer, and close a business meeting using gerund and infinitive verb patterns. Key words: agenda, to chair, objective, attendee, minutes, action item, to recap, takeaway, consensus, on the same page, to assign, to adjourn. Structure: verb + -ing (suggest, recommend) vs verb + to + infinitive (decide, agree, plan); preposition + -ing.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Agenda -- the list of items to be discussed in a meeting (pauta)
"Let's stick to the agenda so we finish on time."
To chair -- to lead and run a meeting (presidir, conduzir)
"Andrea will chair today's meeting."
Objective -- the specific result you want to achieve (objetivo)
"The objective today is to approve the budget."
Attendee -- a person who is present at a meeting (participante, presente)
"We had twelve attendees on the call."
Minutes -- the official written record of a meeting (ata)
"I'll send the minutes by tomorrow morning."
Action item -- a specific task assigned during a meeting (tarefa, ponto de acao)
"Each action item has an owner and a deadline."
To recap -- to summarize the main points quickly (recapitular)
"Before we close, let me recap what we agreed."
Takeaway -- the key point or conclusion to remember (conclusao principal)
"The main takeaway is that demand is rising."
Consensus -- general agreement across the group (consenso)
"We reached consensus on the new rates."
On the same page -- in full agreement, sharing one understanding (alinhados)
"Let's make sure everyone is on the same page."
To assign -- to give a task or responsibility to someone (atribuir, designar)
"I'll assign the follow-up to the sales team."
To adjourn -- to formally close or end a meeting (encerrar, suspender)
"If there's nothing else, we can adjourn."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Agenda
To chair
Objective
Attendee
Minutes
Action item
To recap
Takeaway
Consensus
On the same page
To assign
To adjourn

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Andrea chairs the meeting. 'The objective today is to approve the new rates,' she says, 'so let's stick to the agenda.' After the discussion, the group reaches consensus, and everyone is on the same page. 'I suggest reviewing the final numbers by email,' Andrea adds, 'and we agreed to send a draft by Thursday.' Before she adjourns, she recaps the main takeaway and confirms each action item. 'I'll assign the follow-up to my team, and I'll send the minutes to every attendee. Thank you all for coming.'

1. 'I suggest reviewing the numbers' uses which form, and why?
A The -ing form -- 'suggest' is always followed by a gerund.
B To + infinitive -- 'suggest' is followed by 'to'.
C Present simple -- it is a fact.
2. Which verb is followed by to + infinitive?
A suggest
B agree
C avoid
3. Which sentence is correct?
A Thank you for coming to the meeting.
B Thank you for come to the meeting.
C Thank you for to come to the meeting.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Gerund vs Infinitive

GRAMMAR

Como escolher entre -ing e to + infinitivo depois de certos verbos (explicacao em ingles e portugues).

FormUse / UsoExample
Verb + -ingapos suggest, recommend, avoid, consider, keep. after these verbs, use the -ing form.I suggest reviewing the budget.
Verb + to + infinitiveapos decide, agree, plan, need, offer, hope. after these verbs, use to + infinitive.We agreed to meet again.
Preposition + -ingapos qualquer preposicao (for, before, after, 'look forward to'). always -ing.Thanks for coming.
Regra de ouro / Golden ruleaprenda o verbo junto com seu padrao. learn the verb with its pattern.suggest + -ing vs decide + to.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"I suggest the numbers first."
"We agreed the decision."
"Thank you all today."
"Before we adjourn, let me the takeaways."
"Let's stick to the so we finish on time."
"Each has an owner and a deadline."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma reuniao bem conduzida na ordem correta.

?Recap the takeaways and confirm each action item.
?Send a short agenda and state one clear objective.
?Thank the attendees, promise the minutes, and adjourn.
?Reach consensus, or get the group on the same page.
?Keep the discussion on track and invite everyone to speak.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

The objective today is to approve the new rates.
O objetivo hoje e aprovar as novas tarifas.
I suggest reviewing the numbers before we vote.
Sugiro revisar os numeros antes de votarmos.
We agreed to send a draft by Thursday.
Concordamos em enviar um rascunho ate quinta.
Before we adjourn, let me recap the takeaways.
Antes de encerrarmos, deixe-me recapitular os pontos.
Thank you all for coming. Meeting adjourned.
Obrigada a todos por virem. Reuniao encerrada.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

You want to propose starting with the budget. You say:
A I suggest starting with the budget.
B I suggest to start with the budget.
C I suggest start with the budget.
You confirm the group's decision to meet again. You say:
A We agreed to meet again next week.
B We agreed meeting again next week.
C We agreed meet again next week.
You thank the attendees at the end. You say:
A Thank you all for coming.
B Thank you all for to come.
C Thank you all for come.
A topic needs more work. You say:
A I suggest moving it to the next meeting.
B I suggest to move it to the next meeting.
C I suggest move it to the next meeting.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you are chairing the final meeting about next season's rates for Escape Turismo. Record a one-minute wrap-up. Open by stating the objective, recap one takeaway ('the main takeaway is...'), use 'I suggest + -ing' for a next step, use 'we agreed to + infinitive' to confirm a decision, assign one action item with a deadline, thank the attendees ('thank you for coming'), and adjourn. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1The objective today is to approve the new rates.O objetivo hoje e aprovar as novas tarifas.
2Let's stick to the agenda so we finish on time.Vamos seguir a pauta para terminarmos no horario.
3Before we adjourn, let me recap the main takeaways.Antes de encerrarmos, deixe-me recapitular os pontos principais.
4I'll assign the follow-up to my team.Vou atribuir o acompanhamento a minha equipe.
5Thank you all for coming. Meeting adjourned.Obrigada a todos por virem. Reuniao encerrada.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Analyzing Results and Making Deductions

How to read business results and deduce the cause using past modals of deduction. Key words: figures, target, to exceed, to underperform, downturn, to bounce back, root cause, to account for, assumption, benchmark, margin, quarter. Structure: must have + pp (near certainty) vs might/could have + pp (possible) vs can't have + pp (near certainty it did not happen).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Figures -- the numbers or data in a report (numeros, dados)
"The quarterly figures are on the second slide."
Target -- the specific number or goal you aim to reach (meta)
"We set an ambitious target for the summer."
To exceed -- to go beyond a target or expectation (superar, exceder)
"Bookings exceeded the target by ten percent."
To underperform -- to do worse than expected (ter desempenho abaixo do esperado)
"The city hotels underperformed this quarter."
Downturn -- a fall in business activity or sales (queda, retracao)
"The downturn hit corporate travel hardest."
To bounce back -- to recover after a difficult period (se recuperar)
"Leisure travel bounced back in June."
Root cause -- the underlying reason something happened (causa raiz)
"The root cause was a pricing error."
To account for -- to explain the reason for something (explicar, justificar)
"The heatwave accounts for the drop in July."
Assumption -- something you believe is true without proof (suposicao, premissa)
"That was an assumption, not a fact."
Benchmark -- a standard used to compare performance (referencia, parametro)
"We measure ourselves against the industry benchmark."
Margin -- the difference between cost and selling price (margem de lucro)
"Discounts cut into our margin."
Quarter -- a three-month period in the business year (trimestre)
"Revenue rose in the third quarter."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Figures
Target
To exceed
To underperform
Downturn
To bounce back
Root cause
To account for
Assumption
Benchmark
Margin
Quarter

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Andrea reviews the quarter's figures with her team. Leisure exceeded the target, so the campaign must have worked. Corporate underperformed, but since the whole market saw a downturn, the company can't have caused the drop alone. The margin was thinner, so the discounts might have been too deep -- though Andrea calls that an assumption, not a root cause. 'That accounts for part of it,' she says, 'but let's test it against the benchmark before we act. Leisure bounced back; now we find out why corporate didn't.'

1. 'The campaign must have worked' shows the speaker is:
A almost certain it happened.
B only guessing it is possible.
C sure it did NOT happen.
2. Which form means 'I am almost certain it did NOT happen'?
A might have + pp
B can't have + pp
C must have + pp
3. Which sentence is correct?
A The weather might have affected demand.
B The weather might affected demand.
C The weather might have affect demand.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Modals of Deduction

GRAMMAR

Como deduzir a causa de um resultado passado (explicacao em ingles e portugues).

FormUse / UsoExample
must have + ppquase certeza de que aconteceu. near certainty it happened.Sales rose -- it must have worked.
might / may / could have + pppossibilidade, sem certeza. possible, not sure.The weather might have affected it.
can't / couldn't have + ppquase certeza de que NAO aconteceu. near certainty it did NOT happen.They can't have seen the rates.
Regra de ouro / Golden rulesempre modal + have + participio. always modal + have + past participle.must / might / can't + have + done.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"Sales rose, so the campaign worked."
"The weather affected demand."
"The whole market fell, so we caused it alone."
"Leisure the target this quarter."
"Let's find the before we act."
"That is an , not a fact."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma boa analise de resultados na ordem correta.

?Read the figures and compare them against the target.
?Confirm the root cause before you decide what to do next.
?Check the results against the market benchmark.
?Deduce the likely cause with must have, might have, or can't have.
?Separate the facts from your assumptions.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

The campaign must have driven the growth.
A campanha deve ter impulsionado o crescimento.
The weather might have affected demand.
O clima pode ter afetado a demanda.
We can't have caused the drop on our own.
Nao podemos ter causado a queda sozinhos.
Leisure exceeded the target this quarter.
O lazer superou a meta neste trimestre.
Let's find the root cause before we act.
Vamos encontrar a causa raiz antes de agir.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A segment beat its target and you're almost sure why. You say:
A The campaign must have worked.
B The campaign must worked.
C The campaign must have work.
A number dropped and you think a cause is possible. You say:
A The weather might have affected it.
B The weather might affected it.
C The weather might have affect it.
You rule out a cause you're almost sure did not happen. You say:
A They can't have seen the new rates.
B They can't seen the new rates.
C They can't have saw the new rates.
You describe a segment that did worse than expected. You say:
A The corporate segment underperformed.
B The corporate segment exceeded.
C The corporate segment benchmark.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine the third-quarter figures for Escape Turismo have just landed: leisure exceeded the target, corporate underperformed, and the margin was thinner. Record a one-minute analysis for the board. State one fact, make one strong deduction with 'must have', offer one possible cause with 'might have' or 'could have', rule out one cause with 'can't have', and name the root cause you want to test against the benchmark. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1The campaign must have driven the growth.A campanha deve ter impulsionado o crescimento.
2The weather might have affected demand.O clima pode ter afetado a demanda.
3We can't have caused the drop on our own.Nao podemos ter causado a queda sozinhos.
4Let's find the root cause before we act.Vamos encontrar a causa raiz antes de agir.
5That's an assumption -- let's test it against the benchmark.Isso e uma suposicao -- vamos testa-la contra a referencia.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Giving and Responding to Feedback

How to give and take feedback that is candid, kind, and clear, using cleft sentences for emphasis. Key words: constructive, candid, to commend, shortcoming, to pinpoint, actionable, to take on board, defensive, blind spot, tactful, to come across, praise. Structure: What + clause + is/was + X and It + is/was + X + that/who to spotlight the key point.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Constructive -- meant to help someone improve, not just to criticize (construtivo)
"She gave constructive feedback after the pitch."
Candid -- honest and direct, even about difficult things (franco, sincero)
"I'll be candid: the opening ran too long."
To commend -- to praise someone, often formally, for what they did well (elogiar, parabenizar)
"I want to commend you on the client research."
Shortcoming -- a weakness or fault in someone's work (deficiencia, ponto fraco)
"The one shortcoming was the unclear pricing slide."
To pinpoint -- to identify something exactly (identificar com precisao)
"Let me pinpoint what to change next time."
Actionable -- clear and specific enough to act on (acionavel, pratico)
"Good feedback is specific and actionable."
To take on board -- to accept advice and seriously consider it (levar em conta, absorver)
"I'll take your comments on board for the next meeting."
Defensive -- protecting yourself from criticism instead of listening (defensivo, na defensiva)
"Try not to get defensive when you hear feedback."
Blind spot -- a weakness in yourself that you cannot easily see (ponto cego)
"Speaking too fast is a blind spot of mine."
Tactful -- careful not to offend when saying something difficult (diplomatico, com tato)
"She was tactful about the mistake."
To come across -- to appear or seem in a particular way (passar a impressao, soar)
"You came across as confident and prepared."
Praise -- words that express approval or admiration (elogio)
"Balance your praise with one clear suggestion."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Constructive
Candid
To commend
Shortcoming
To pinpoint
Actionable
To take on board
Defensive
Blind spot
Tactful
To come across
Praise

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

After a client pitch, Andrea gives Felix constructive feedback. She is candid but kind. First she wants to commend him: 'What impressed me most was your preparation.' Then she names one shortcoming. 'It was the pricing slide that lost them for a moment,' she says, 'and that's the point I'd pinpoint.' Her suggestion is actionable: 'What you could work on is the close.' Felix doesn't get defensive; he simply says he'll take it on board. Both know that everyone has a blind spot, and that good feedback, delivered with tact, is how you come across as a leader worth learning from.

1. 'What impressed me was your research' puts the emphasis on:
A your research.
B me.
C nothing in particular.
2. In an it-cleft, which word joins the two halves?
A what
B that or who
C was
3. Which sentence is correct?
A What you could work on is the close.
B What you could work the close.
C What you could work on the close.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Cleft Sentences for Emphasis

GRAMMAR

Como destacar o ponto mais importante ao dar feedback (explicacao em ingles e portugues).

FormUse / UsoExample
What + clause + is / was + Xdestaca X (o ponto-chave). emphasizes X, the key point.What impressed me was your prep.
It + is / was + X + that / whodestaca X com forca. strongly emphasizes X.It was your clarity that won them.
What ... could do is + Vfoca uma sugestao. focuses a suggestion.What you could do is shorten it.
Regra de ouro / Golden rulecleft precisa do verbo de ligacao + that/who. cleft needs its linking verb + that/who.What matters is... / It was X that...

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"What impressed me your preparation."
"It was your clarity made the difference."
"I want to you on the research."
"The one Id pinpoint is the pricing slide."
"Ill take your comments on for next time."
"Good feedback is candid but ."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de um bom feedback na ordem correta.

?Use a cleft sentence to spotlight the key point.
?Check the other person has taken it on board.
?Open with genuine praise for one real strength.
?Pinpoint a single shortcoming, not a long list.
?Make your suggestion specific and actionable.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

What impressed me most was your preparation.
O que mais me impressionou foi a sua preparacao.
It was your clarity that made the difference.
Foi a sua clareza que fez a diferenca.
What you could work on is the close.
O que voce poderia melhorar e o fechamento.
That's fair -- I'll take it on board.
E justo -- vou levar isso em conta.
Good feedback is candid, but tactful.
Um bom feedback e franco, mas com tato.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

You want to praise a colleague and spotlight one strength. You say:
A What impressed me was your preparation.
B What impressed me your preparation.
C What impressed me is were your preparation.
You emphasize the one thing that won the client over. You say:
A It was your clarity that made the difference.
B It was your clarity what made the difference.
C It was your clarity made the difference.
You give an actionable suggestion for the close. You say:
A What you could work on is the close.
B What you could work the close.
C What you could working on is the close.
A colleague criticizes your slide and you respond well. You say:
A That's fair -- I'll take it on board.
B No, you're completely wrong about that.
C That's not my fault, end of story.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine a member of your team at Escape Turismo has just delivered a client pitch. Record a one-minute piece of feedback for them. Open with genuine praise using a 'What impressed me was...' cleft, emphasize one thing that worked with 'It was ... that ...', then pinpoint a single shortcoming and turn it into an actionable suggestion with 'What you could work on is...'. Keep it candid but tactful. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1What impressed me most was your preparation.O que mais me impressionou foi a sua preparacao.
2It was your clarity that made the difference.Foi a sua clareza que fez a diferenca.
3What you could work on is the close.O que voce poderia melhorar e o fechamento.
4That's fair -- I'll take it on board.E justo -- vou levar isso em conta.
5Good feedback is candid, but tactful.Um bom feedback e franco, mas com tato.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Coaching and Developing Your Team

How to delegate, empower, and develop your team using causative have and get. Key words: to delegate, to empower, to mentor, ownership, potential, to nurture, hands-off, to step up, accountability, to micromanage, autonomy, learning curve. Structure: have + person + base verb; get + person + to + verb; have / get + object + past participle.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

To delegate -- to give a task or responsibility to someone else (delegar)
"A good leader learns to delegate, not to do it all."
To empower -- to give someone the authority and confidence to act (empoderar, dar autonomia)
"I want to empower my team to make their own calls."
To mentor -- to guide and advise a less experienced colleague (orientar, ser mentor de)
"She mentored me through my first big negotiation."
Ownership -- the feeling of being responsible for a task or result (senso de dono, responsabilidade)
"Give people ownership and they rise to it."
Potential -- ability that has not yet been fully developed (potencial)
"He has the potential to lead the whole account."
To nurture -- to care for someone and help them grow (cultivar, desenvolver)
"You nurture talent by trusting it with real work."
Hands-off -- giving people freedom to work without close control (nao intervencionista)
"My style is hands-off once I trust the person."
To step up -- to take on more responsibility when it is needed (assumir o desafio, dar um passo a frente)
"She stepped up the moment I gave her the chance."
Accountability -- being responsible for results and answerable for them (responsabilizacao, prestacao de contas)
"Ownership means accountability, not just credit."
To micromanage -- to control every small detail of someone's work (microgerenciar)
"If you micromanage, people never learn."
Autonomy -- the freedom to make your own decisions (autonomia)
"Autonomy is how people grow into leaders."
Learning curve -- how quickly someone must learn something new (curva de aprendizado)
"It's a steep learning curve, but I'll support him."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

To delegate
To empower
To mentor
Ownership
Potential
To nurture
Hands-off
To step up
Accountability
To micromanage
Autonomy
Learning curve

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Andrea decides to delegate the supplier negotiations to Oskar. Instead of leading them herself, she will have him run the whole process, to give him real ownership. 'You have the potential,' she tells him, 'I just need to empower you to use it.' She promises to mentor him but stay hands-off: 'I'd rather get you to make one small mistake than micromanage you.' To help him prepare, she had the rate sheets forwarded that morning. Oskar knows it's a steep learning curve, but with autonomy and support, he's ready to step up.

1. In 'I'll have you run the process', the verb after 'have you' is:
A the base verb (run), with no 'to'.
B the past participle (run/ran).
C 'to run'.
2. Which sentence uses the causative for a THING done for you?
A I had the rate sheets forwarded.
B I had him lead the call.
C I got her to sign it.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A I got him to take ownership.
B I got him take ownership.
C I had him to take ownership.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Causative: Have & Get

GRAMMAR

Como falar de mandar/fazer alguem fazer algo, e providenciar que algo seja feito (em ingles e portugues).

FormUse / UsoExample
have + person + base verbdelegar a alguem (sem 'to'). delegate to a person (no 'to').I had her present it.
get + person + to + verbconvencer/pedir a alguem (com 'to'). persuade a person (with 'to').I got him to lead it.
have / get + object + participioprovidenciar que algo seja feito. arrange for a thing to be done.I had the report reviewed.
Regra de ouro / Golden rule'have' nao usa 'to' antes da pessoa; 'get' usa. have = no 'to'; get = 'to'.have her go / get her to go.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"This quarter, Ill have you the whole process."
"I want to get you ownership of this."
"I had the rate sheets this morning."
"A good leader learns to ."
"Ill stay and let you decide."
"Give people and they rise to it."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma boa delegacao na ordem correta.

?Stay hands-off, but be available for the hard questions.
?Have them run the process and make the decisions.
?Choose a task that will stretch and grow the person.
?Debrief the learning curve and set them up to do it again.
?Hand over real ownership, not just the task.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

I'm going to have you run the whole process.
Vou fazer voce conduzir todo o processo.
I want to get you to take ownership of this.
Quero que voce assuma a responsabilidade por isto.
I had the rate sheets forwarded to you.
Mandei encaminhar as tabelas de tarifas para voce.
I'll mentor you, but I'll stay hands-off.
Vou te orientar, mas nao vou interferir.
Step up on this one, and I'll be right behind you.
Assuma este desafio, e estarei logo atras de voce.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

You delegate a task to a person on your team. You say:
A I'll have you lead the call.
B I'll have you to lead the call.
C I'll have you led the call.
You persuade a colleague to take a decision. You say:
A I want to get you to decide this.
B I want to get you decide this.
C I want to have you to decide this.
You arrange for a report to be reviewed by someone else. You say:
A I'll have the report reviewed.
B I'll have the report review.
C I'll have reviewed the report.
You describe controlling every small detail of someone's work. You call it:
A to micromanage.
B to empower.
C to delegate.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine there is one responsibility you currently handle yourself at Escape Turismo that a member of your team could grow into -- a negotiation, a client account, a report. Record a one-minute coaching plan. Say who you will have take it on, using 'have + person + verb'; how you will empower them with 'get + person + to + verb'; and one thing you will have done for them ('have / get + object + past participle'). Explain how you'll stay hands-off but supportive. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1I'm going to have you run the whole process.Vou fazer voce conduzir todo o processo.
2I want to get you to take ownership of this.Quero que voce assuma a responsabilidade por isto.
3I'll mentor you, but I'll stay hands-off.Vou te orientar, mas nao vou interferir.
4I had the rate sheets forwarded to you.Mandei encaminhar as tabelas de tarifas para voce.
5Step up on this one, and I'll be right behind you.Assuma este desafio, e estarei logo atras de voce.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Handling High-Stakes Conversations

How to stay composed under pressure, de-escalate, and hold your ground -- using mixed conditionals. Key words: high-stakes, composure, to de-escalate, pushback, to stand your ground, non-negotiable, to concede, ultimatum, deadlock, to walk away, to defuse, to salvage. Structure: past 'if' + present result (had done ... would be); present 'if' + past result (were/did ... would have done).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

High-stakes -- with a lot to win or lose; where the outcome really matters (de alto risco, com muito em jogo)
"This is a high-stakes call -- the whole account depends on it."
Composure -- calmness and self-control under pressure (compostura, autocontrole)
"She kept her composure even when he raised his voice."
To de-escalate -- to make a tense situation calmer (desescalar, acalmar a tensao)
"I tried to de-escalate before anyone said something they'd regret."
Pushback -- strong resistance or opposition to a plan or idea (resistencia, oposicao)
"We got serious pushback on the new cancellation terms."
To stand your ground -- to refuse to change your position under pressure (manter sua posicao)
"I stood my ground on the deposit -- it protects us."
Non-negotiable -- that cannot be changed or discussed (inegociavel)
"The insurance clause is non-negotiable for us."
To concede -- to admit a point or give something up in an argument (ceder, conceder um ponto)
"I conceded on the dates so I could hold firm on price."
Ultimatum -- a final demand with a threat if it is refused (ultimato)
"He gave me an ultimatum: sign today or lose the rate."
Deadlock -- a situation where no agreement is possible (impasse)
"We reached a deadlock and agreed to sleep on it."
To walk away -- to leave a deal rather than accept bad terms (abandonar a negociacao, desistir)
"If they won't move, I'm prepared to walk away."
To defuse -- to remove the tension or danger from a situation (neutralizar a tensao)
"A calm question can defuse a heated moment."
To salvage -- to save something from a difficult or failing situation (salvar, recuperar)
"We salvaged the relationship even though the deal fell through."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

High-stakes
Composure
To de-escalate
Pushback
To stand your ground
Non-negotiable
To concede
Ultimatum
Deadlock
To walk away
To defuse
To salvage

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

A supplier, Klaus, gives Andrea an ultimatum: match the rate today or move the group. Instead of fighting, she keeps her composure and works to de-escalate. 'If we had locked in last season's rate,' she reflects, 'we wouldn't be at this deadlock now.' She meets the pushback calmly: she can concede on payment terms, but the cancellation protection is non-negotiable -- 'if I dropped it, I'd be exposing both of us.' She knows her limit and is prepared to walk away: 'If I were the kind of supplier who caved, you wouldn't have trusted us for six years.' Even if the deal falls through, she wants to salvage the relationship.

1. In 'If we had locked in the rate, we wouldn't be stuck now', the result clause is:
A present -- 'would + base verb' (wouldn't be).
B past -- 'would have + participle'.
C future -- 'will be'.
2. Which sentence mixes a present fact with a past result?
A If I were more assertive, I would have pushed back.
B If I had prepared, I would be calmer now.
C If I prepare, I will be calmer.
3. Which sentence is correct for a past cause and a present result?
A If I had set the boundary, I would be calmer now.
B If I had set the boundary, I would have been calmer now.
C If I set the boundary, I would be calmer now.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Mixed Conditionals

GRAMMAR

Como juntar dois tempos numa so frase -- passado que afeta o presente, e presente que explica o passado (em ingles e portugues).

FormUse / UsoExample
Past → presentcausa no passado, resultado agora. past cause, present result.If I had prepared, I would be calmer now.
Present → pastfato permanente, resultado no passado. present fact, past result.If I were braver, I would have said no.
As formas / The formspast perfect + would + base verb; OU past simple/were + would have + participio.had done ... would be / were ... would have done.
Regra de ouro / Golden ruleolhe cada clausula sozinha e de a ela o tempo a que pertence. read each clause on its own.nao force tudo num so tempo.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"If we the rate, we wouldn't be stuck now."
"If I had prepared better, I calmer now."
"If I more composed, I would have handled it better."
"If she weren't so calm, she the deal."
"This clause is for us."
"I'm prepared to if the terms are wrong."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma conversa de alto risco na ordem correta.

?When the ultimatum lands, keep your composure and de-escalate.
?Concede a flexible point, then stand your ground on the non-negotiable.
?Ask a calm question to defuse the heat and slow the pace.
?Before the meeting, decide what is non-negotiable and what you can concede.
?State your walk-away point calmly, and protect the relationship either way.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

I hear you -- but not under an ultimatum.
Eu te entendo, mas nao sob um ultimato.
Let me ask one thing before we go there.
Deixe-me perguntar uma coisa antes de irmos por esse caminho.
If we had settled this earlier, we wouldn't be stuck now.
Se tivessemos resolvido isto antes, nao estariamos travados agora.
This point is non-negotiable for us.
Este ponto e inegociavel para nos.
I'm prepared to walk away, and that's just my limit.
Estou preparada para desistir, e isso e apenas o meu limite.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A client opens with a take-it-or-leave-it demand. You de-escalate. You say:
A I hear you -- let me ask one thing before we go there.
B That's ridiculous. Take it or leave it yourself.
C Fine, whatever you want.
You explain how a past decision left you stuck now. You say:
A If we had settled this earlier, we wouldn't be at a deadlock now.
B If we had settled this earlier, we wouldn't have been at a deadlock now.
C If we settle this earlier, we won't be at a deadlock.
You explain a past outcome with a present fact about yourself. You say:
A If I were less patient, I would have given him an ultimatum.
B If I was less patient, I would give him an ultimatum.
C If I am less patient, I will give him an ultimatum.
You describe refusing to change your position under pressure. You call it:
A standing your ground.
B walking away.
C conceding.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine a real high-stakes conversation you might face at Escape Turismo -- a supplier's ultimatum, a client threatening to leave, a partner pushing back hard. Record a one-minute plan for handling it. Say how you'll keep your composure and de-escalate; name one non-negotiable and one point you could concede; and use at least one mixed conditional to reflect ('If we had..., we wouldn't be... now' or 'If I were..., I would have...'). Finish with your walk-away point, stated calmly. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1I hear you -- but not under an ultimatum.Eu te entendo, mas nao sob um ultimato.
2Let me ask one thing before we go there.Deixe-me perguntar uma coisa antes de irmos por esse caminho.
3This point is non-negotiable for us.Este ponto e inegociavel para nos.
4If we had settled this earlier, we wouldn't be stuck now.Se tivessemos resolvido isto antes, nao estariamos travados agora.
5I'm prepared to walk away, and that's just my limit.Estou preparada para desistir, e isso e apenas o meu limite.

IN CLASS -- Selecione a Aula

01
Reactivation Diagnostic -- Who Are You in English?
Reactivating your English at an international tourism fair -- 32 slides
02
Back in the Game -- Your Professional Identity
Describing your role and current projects at the agency -- 28 slides
03
The Art of Small Talk -- Conversations at the Fair
Starting and sustaining conversations at international fairs -- 32 slides
04
Talking About Your World -- Trips & Highlights
Describing past experiences, trips and professional highlights -- 28 slides
05
What's Next -- Plans, Goals & Commitments
Talking about plans, goals and future commitments -- 32 slides
06
Making It Stick -- Follow-Ups & Relationships
Networking language, follow-ups and long-term relationships -- 28 slides
07
Listening and Responding -- Native Speakers at Fairs
Understanding fast native speakers and clarifying in real time -- 25 slides
08
Midpoint Review -- How Far You Have Come
A midpoint review of your journey: past, present, and future together -- 28 slides
09
Talking Prices, Packages, and Deals
Quote prices, build packages, and negotiate deals using the first conditional -- 28 slides
10
Advising Clients and Handling Tricky Requests
Advise clients and handle difficult requests using the second conditional -- 28 slides
11
Handling Complaints and Difficult Situations
Handle complaints and service problems professionally using the passive voice -- 36 slides
12
Negotiating Partnerships and Contracts
Negotiate terms and report what was agreed using reported speech -- 36 slides
13
Presenting Your Company at a Trade Fair
Pitch your company and describe it with relative clauses -- 36 slides
14
Talking About Trends and the Future of Tourism
Discuss trends and forecast the future with future continuous and future perfect -- 36 slides
15
Leading and Wrapping Up a Business Meeting
Chair a meeting and close it well using gerund and infinitive verb patterns -- 36 slides
16
Analyzing Results and Making Deductions
Review results and deduce the cause with past modals of deduction -- 36 slides
17
Giving and Responding to Feedback
Give and take feedback clearly using cleft sentences for emphasis -- 36 slides
18
Coaching and Developing Your Team
Delegate and empower your team using causative have and get -- 36 slides
19
Handling High-Stakes Conversations
Stay composed under pressure using mixed conditionals -- 36 slides

Atividades Complementares

Materiais extras para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído após assistir/ouvir.

Lesson 1 — Reactivation Diagnostic -- Who Are You in English?

Series
Somebody Feed Phil -- Any Episode (Netflix)

Phil travels the world, meets hoteliers, chefs and locals, and has warm, natural conversations everywhere he goes. It is your world — hospitality, destinations, small talk — in easy, real English. Connection to Lesson 1: notice how he introduces himself, asks questions, and keeps a conversation flowing.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 phrases Phil uses to start a conversation with someone new.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 332: Don't Hit a Brick Wall When Asking Someone About Their Job

A Business English episode from the All Ears English teachers on how to make small talk and keep a conversation going when you ask someone about their work, modeling exactly the fluency and spontaneity you are reactivating.

Note two follow-up questions from the episode and try them in your next work chat.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
"Present Perfect vs Past Simple" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on the exact contrast from today: experience and duration up to now (present perfect, for/since) versus finished moments in the past (past simple). Connection to Lesson 1: the grammar that lets you talk about your twenty years in tourism with confidence and precision.

Tip: pause after each example and say one sentence about your own career — one with present perfect, one with past simple.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Back in the Game -- Your Professional Identity

Series
The Parisian Agency: Exclusive Properties -- Any Episode (Netflix)

A real family agency describes its portfolio, manages clients, and works on new deals every episode. It is your professional world in English — roles, routines, and projects in motion. Connection to Lesson 2: notice how they describe what they do (present simple) and what they are working on right now (present continuous).

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences where someone describes a current project ("we are working on...", "right now we are...").

Watch ↗
Podcast
The Travel Marketing Podcast — How Welcome Pickups Scale to 300+ Destinations

Savvas Georgiou, co-founder and Chief Product Officer of Welcome Pickups, introduces his role and describes how the company grew its airport-transfer service to 300+ destinations. Exactly the language of presenting your job and current projects.

Note how the guest introduces his title and company, then draft a one-line intro of your own role.

Open episode ↗
YouTube
"Present Simple vs Present Continuous" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on today's exact contrast: permanent facts and routines (present simple) versus temporary things happening around now (present continuous). Connection to Lesson 2: the grammar that lets you describe your role and your current projects without hesitating.

Tip: pause after each example and say one sentence about your own work — one in the present simple, one in the present continuous.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — The Art of Small Talk -- Conversations at the Fair

Series
Somebody Feed Phil -- Any Episode (Netflix)

Phil is a master of small talk. In every city he breaks the ice with strangers, asks warm follow-up questions, and finds common ground over food and travel. It is exactly the natural, friendly English you need at a fair. Connection to Lesson 3: watch how he opens and sustains a conversation with someone he has just met.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 questions Phil uses to keep a conversation going.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 332: Don't Hit a Brick Wall When Asking Someone About Their Job

A Business English episode from the All Ears English teachers on how to make small talk and keep a conversation going when you ask someone about their work, modeling exactly the fluency and spontaneity you are reactivating.

Note two follow-up questions from the episode and try them in your next work chat.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
"Are You Big on Small Talk?" -- 6 Minute English (BBC Learning English)

A short BBC discussion episode all about small talk: why it matters, the openers people use, and how to keep a friendly conversation going. Connection to Lesson 3: the natural language and questions that let you start and sustain any conversation at a fair.

Tip: pause after each example and say the question out loud, then invent one of your own with Do, Are, or Have.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Talking About Your World -- Trips & Highlights

Series
Down to Earth with Zac Efron -- Any Episode (Netflix)

Zac travels the world and, at the end of each trip, tells the story of what he saw and learned — all in natural past-tense English. It is a perfect model for narrating a journey. Connection to Lesson 4: notice how he describes where he went, what happened, and how each experience turned out.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 5 past-simple verbs (2 regular, 3 irregular) that he uses to tell a story.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Zero To Travel — A Race Around the World, Meeting Strangers Abroad, and Storytelling With Adrien Behn

Podcaster Adrien Behn tells the story of Nellie Bly and Elizabeth Bisland's 1889 race around the globe and talks about the art of travel storytelling and finding your voice. Rich, natural past-tense narration.

Notice how the guest uses past tenses to tell a story, then pause and retell one anecdote in your own words.

Open episode ↗
YouTube
"Past Simple: Regular and Irregular Verbs" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on today's exact grammar: regular -ed endings, irregular past forms, and how to make questions and negatives with 'did'. Connection to Lesson 4: the grammar that lets you tell any story about a trip with confidence and accuracy.

Tip: pause after each example and tell one sentence about your own last trip, then turn it into a question with 'did'.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — What's Next -- Plans, Goals & Commitments

Series
The Parisian Agency: Exclusive Properties -- Any Episode (Netflix)

In every episode the agents plan their next moves: which clients they are going to call, which properties they will show, and the meetings they are already scheduling. It is the future in action, in professional English. Connection to Lesson 5: notice how they mix 'going to', 'will', and present continuous when they plan.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences about future plans, one for each form (going to, will, -ing).

Watch ↗
Podcast
The Travel Marketing Podcast — From Content Creation to Conversion: Inside Contiki's Marketing Playbook

Contiki CMO Lottie Norman talks about plans and trends for the seasons ahead: emerging channels like TikTok, ambassador programs, and how they will grow their audience. Ideal language for talking about future goals in travel.

Listen for future-plan language ('we're going to', 'the next step is') and note the trends she names.

Open episode ↗
YouTube
"Will vs Going To vs Present Continuous" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on today's exact grammar: when to use 'will', 'going to', and the present continuous for the future. Connection to Lesson 5: the three future forms that let you plan, commit, and negotiate the next season with confidence.

Tip: pause after each example and say one sentence about your own plans — one with 'going to', one with 'will', one with the present continuous.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Making It Stick -- Follow-Ups & Relationships

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix / streaming)

The masters of the follow-up. Watch how the lawyers turn one meeting into a lasting relationship: they make polite requests, warm offers, and clear recommendations, and they always look for the mutual benefit. Connection to Lesson 6: listen for 'could', 'would', and 'should' in every negotiation.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 polite requests or offers you hear (Could you...? / I would... / You should...).

Watch ↗
Podcast
The Art of Charm — Marsha Shandur: Saying Yes to Networking (Episode 359)

Networking coach Marsha Shandur explains how to follow up properly after meeting someone and turn first contacts into long-lasting professional relationships. Practical, real advice on staying in touch.

Write down one follow-up phrase from the episode and use it after your next meeting.

Open episode ↗
YouTube
"How to Be Polite in English" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical BBC lesson on polite language: softening requests with 'could' and 'would', making warm offers, and sounding professional rather than blunt. Connection to Lesson 6: the polite modals that turn a cold contact into a warm, professional relationship.

Tip: pause after each example and make your own follow-up sentence — one with 'could', one with 'would', one with 'should'.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Listening and Responding -- Native Speakers at Fairs

Series
Ted Lasso -- Season 1, Episode 1 (Apple TV)

An American coach lands in England and must understand fast British English, slang, and strong accents in real time. Watch how he asks people to repeat, checks meaning, and never loses the thread. Connection to Lesson 7: real-time listening and clarification across accents.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 moments where a character asks for clarification or repeats to confirm.

Watch ↗
Podcast
All Ears English -- "How to Understand Fast Native Speakers"

A podcast made for learners who struggle with connected speech and fast native English. The hosts explain linking, reductions, and exactly how to ask for repetition politely. Connection to Lesson 7: the clarification phrases you practiced today.

Tip: listen twice. Write down 5 clarification phrases the hosts use to check understanding.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"How to Understand Native Speakers When They Talk Quickly" -- BBC Learning English

A BBC live English class on following fast native speech: linking, reductions, and how to catch meaning and ask people to slow down or repeat in real time. Connection to Lesson 7: the strategies that keep a fast conversation under your control.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version out loud, once with each phrase.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Midpoint Review -- How Far You Have Come

Series
The Last Dance -- Episode 1 (Netflix)

A documentary about looking back on a long journey and taking stock of progress, milestones, and setbacks at the highest level. Watch how the athletes and coaches reflect on the past, the present, and what is coming next. Connection to Lesson 8: describing a journey using past, present, and future.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 sentences that talk about the past and 3 that talk about future plans.

Watch ↗
Podcast
6 Minute English -- "Talking About Progress and Goals" (BBC)

A short, learner-friendly podcast where the hosts discuss progress, motivation, and setting goals. Perfect for hearing the past, present, and future tenses used naturally together. Connection to Lesson 8: the language of self-assessment and your journey.

Tip: listen twice. Write down one sentence about the past, one about the present, and one about the future.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Past, Present and Future -- English Tenses Review" -- BBC Learning English

A clear review of the exact grammar of this lesson: past simple for finished actions, present continuous for now, and going to / will for the future. Connection to Lesson 8: telling your story across all three time frames.

Tip: pause after each example and make your own sentence about your English journey in that tense.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Talking Prices, Packages, and Deals

Series
Selling Sunset -- Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

A reality series about high-end agents negotiating big deals. Watch how they quote prices, discuss terms, and trade conditions to close a sale. Connection to Lesson 9: quoting prices and negotiating packages and deals.

Tip: watch one negotiation scene twice. Write down two sentences where someone offers something in exchange for a condition.

Watch ↗
Podcast
6 Minute English -- "The Language of Negotiation" (BBC)

A short, learner-friendly podcast about how English speakers bargain and make deals. Perfect for hearing conditional sentences and price language used naturally. Connection to Lesson 9: the first conditional and the vocabulary of prices and deals.

Tip: listen twice. Note one first-conditional sentence you could use with a real client.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"First Conditional -- If + Present, Will + Verb" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on the exact grammar of this class: the first conditional for real future possibilities. Connection to Lesson 9: trading a condition for an offer in a price negotiation.

Tip: after each example, make your own sentence about a deal at Escape Turismo using the first conditional.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Advising Clients and Handling Tricky Requests

Series
Chef's Table (Netflix) -- any episode

A beautifully filmed series where top chefs explain the choices and trade-offs behind their craft. Listen for how they weigh options and justify their decisions. Connection to Lesson 10: weighing trade-offs and explaining hypothetical choices.

Tip: pick one decision a chef describes. Write a second-conditional sentence about what you would do in their place.

Watch ↗
Podcast
6 Minute English -- "Giving Advice" (BBC)

A short, learner-friendly podcast about how English speakers give advice politely. Perfect for hearing the second conditional and softened suggestions used naturally. Connection to Lesson 10: advising clients with "If I were you, I would...".

Tip: listen twice. Write one piece of advice for a client using the second conditional.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"The Second Conditional -- 6 Minute Grammar" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on the exact grammar of this class: the second conditional for unreal and hypothetical situations. Connection to Lesson 10: giving advice with "If I were you, I would...".

Tip: after each example, make your own sentence advising a client at Escape Turismo using the second conditional.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Handling Complaints and Difficult Situations

Series
The White Lotus (HBO / Max) -- Season 1

A sharp drama set in a luxury resort, full of guests with problems and staff who must handle them with a smile. Listen for how the manager responds to complaints and difficult requests. Connection to Lesson 11: service recovery and handling difficult clients.

Tip: pick one guest complaint and write how you would answer it in the passive voice.

Watch ↗
Podcast
6 Minute English (BBC Learning English)

A short, learner-friendly podcast on everyday English. Choose an episode on complaints, apologies, or customer service to hear the language of this lesson used naturally. Connection to Lesson 11: apologizing and reassuring in professional English.

Tip: listen twice, then write one apology and one reassurance using the passive voice.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"How to use the active and passive voice" -- 6 Minute Grammar, BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on the exact grammar of this class: the passive voice (be + past participle) and when to use it. Connection to Lesson 11: sounding calm and professional with 'the problem is being handled'.

Tip: after each example, make your own passive sentence for handling a complaint at Escape Turismo.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Negotiating Partnerships and Contracts

Series
Suits (Netflix / streaming) -- Season 1

A fast, stylish drama set in a top law firm, packed with negotiations, contracts, and high-stakes deals. Listen for how the lawyers report offers and confirm terms. Connection to Lesson 12: negotiating partnerships and reporting what was said.

Tip: pick one negotiation scene and report it back in reported speech: "He said he would...".

Watch ↗
Podcast
6 Minute English (BBC Learning English)

A short, learner-friendly podcast on everyday and business English. Choose an episode on deals, work, or communication to hear reporting language used naturally. Connection to Lesson 12: confirming and reporting what was agreed.

Tip: after listening, report one thing a speaker said using reported speech.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"How to use reported speech" -- 6 Minute Grammar, BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on the exact grammar of this class: reported speech, backshifting the tense, and reporting questions. Connection to Lesson 12: confirming a deal with 'He said the rate would be fixed'.

Tip: after each example, report a sentence from your own last negotiation at Escape Turismo.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Presenting Your Company at a Trade Fair

Series
Shark Tank (ABC / streaming) -- any season

Founders pitch their companies to investors in minutes, using exactly the language of this lesson: what they do, what makes them stand out, and their track record. Connection to Lesson 13: presenting a company and describing it with relative clauses.

Tip: pick one pitch and note every relative clause the founder uses ("a product that...", "a market where...").

Watch ↗
Podcast
How I Built This with Guy Raz (NPR)

Founders tell the story of the company they built -- how they describe it, position it, and explain what makes it different. Rich, natural business English. Connection to Lesson 13: pitching and describing your company with confidence.

Tip: after an episode, write a one-sentence pitch for that company using a relative clause.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Defining relative clauses" -- 6 Minute Grammar, BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on the exact grammar of this class: who, which, that, where, and the difference between defining and non-defining clauses. Connection to Lesson 13: describing your company with 'an agency that...' and 'Patagonia, which is...'.

Tip: after each example, describe one part of Escape Turismo using the same structure.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Talking About Trends and the Future of Tourism

Series
Down to Earth with Zac Efron (Netflix)

A travel documentary that explores sustainable, eco-friendly, and wellness-focused ways to live and travel -- exactly the trends shaping the future of tourism. Rich, natural English about change and the environment. Connection to Lesson 14: trends, sustainability, and forecasting the future.

Tip: after an episode, write two forecasts about that destination using the future perfect and future continuous.

Watch ↗
Podcast
TED Talks Daily (TED)

Short, idea-rich talks, many about the future, technology, and sustainability. Perfect for hearing forecasting language and future forms used by expert speakers. Connection to Lesson 14: making predictions and talking about trends.

Tip: pick a talk about the future and note every future continuous or future perfect you hear.

Listen ↗
YouTube
